B cells are represented as naïve (IGHD, TCL1A), memory (CD27,CD44), and activated (CD69, CD83) subsets, while plasma cells (JCHAIN) express immunoglobulins such as IgA (IGHA1), IgG (IGHG1), and IgM (IGHM).

The myeloid lineage encompasses monocytes (VCAN, CD14), macrophages (C1QA, C1QB), mast cells (TPSB2, CPA3), and dendritic cells, including classical (CLEC9A, CD1C) and plasmacytoid subsets (IL3RA, GZMB), as well as eosinophils (CLC, CCR3) and neutrophils (CXCR2, FCGR3B).

Specialized populations such as gamma-delta T cells (TRDV1, TRGC1) and innate lymphoid cells (ILCs; IL4I1, KIT) are also depicted, along with immune cell developmental stages, such as progenitors (CD24, AXL) and stem-like cells (LEF1), and cycling states (MKI67, CDK1).

TAN markers (OLR1, PLAU, VEGFA) were highly upregulated in TAN, whereas CXCR2, a marker of mature neutrophils, was highly expressed in blood neutrophils but downregulated in TAN (Figure 4F).

From TAN1, neutrophils diverged into two trajectories: one leading to a putative pro-tumor TAN phenotype (TAN4) characterized by high IL1A expression, a key driver of emergency myelopoiesis that fuels inflammation and immunosuppression in the TME28, and another toward a phenotype defined by high expression of antigen-presentation-associated markers, such as HLA-DRA (TAN2/TAN3) (Figure 4J).
